docs.text: Procedure Description (sha256 string) SHA-256 hash, returns hex (sha512 string) SHA-512 hash, returns hex (sha1 string) SHA-1 hash, returns hex (md5 string) MD5 hash, returns hex

docs.title: HMAC
docs.text: Procedure Description (hmac-sha256 key message) HMAC-SHA256 (hmac-sha512 key message) HMAC-SHA512 (hmac-sha1 key message) HMAC-SHA1 (hmac-md5 key message) HMAC-MD5

docs.title: CSV
docs.text:
(kaappi csv) — CSV parser and writer. Pure Scheme, no build step.

thottam install kaappi-csv

RFC 4180 compliant — handles quoted fields, embedded commas, embedded newlines, and escaped quotes.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi csv))

;; With headers — rows become alists
(csv-read-string "name,age\nAlice,30\nBob,25" 'headers #t)
;=> ((("name" . "Alice") ("age" . "30"))
;    (("name" . "Bob") ("age" . "25")))

;; Without headers — rows are lists of strings
(csv-read-string "a,b,c\n1,2,3")
;=> (("a" "b" "c") ("1" "2" "3"))

docs.title: From a file
docs.text:
(import (scheme base) (scheme file) (kaappi csv))

(define rows
  (call-with-input-file "data.csv"
    (lambda (port) (csv-read port))))

docs.title: With headers
docs.text:
When the first row contains column names, use 'headers #t to get rows as alists:

(csv-read-string "name,age\nAlice,30\nBob,25" 'headers #t)
;=> ((("name" . "Alice") ("age" . "30"))
;    (("name" . "Bob") ("age" . "25")))

;; Access fields by name
(cdr (assoc "name" (car records)))  ;=> "Alice"

docs.title: Custom delimiters
docs.text:
Tab-separated, semicolon-separated, or any character:

(csv-read-string "name\tscore\nAlice\t95" 'delimiter #\tab)
;=> (("name" "score") ("Alice" "95"))

(csv-read-string "a;b;c\n1;2;3" 'delimiter #\;)
;=> (("a" "b" "c") ("1" "2" "3"))

docs.title: One row at a time
docs.text:
(call-with-input-file "data.csv"
  (lambda (port)
    (let loop ((row (csv-read-row port)))
      (when row
        (display row) (newline)
        (loop (csv-read-row port))))))

csv-read-row returns #f at end of file.

docs.title: To a file
docs.text:
(call-with-output-file "output.csv"
  (lambda (port)
    (csv-write '(("name" "age") ("Alice" "30") ("Bob" "25")) port)))

docs.title: To a string
docs.text:
(csv-write-string '(("name" "score") ("Alice" "95") ("Bob" "87")))
;=> "name,score
Alice,95
Bob,87
"

docs.title: Write a single row
docs.text: (csv-write-row '("Alice" "30" "Berlin") port)

docs.title: Custom delimiter in output
docs.text:
(csv-write-string '(("a" "b") ("1" "2")) 'delimiter #\tab)
;=> "a\tb\r\n1\t2\r\n"

docs.title: Type coercion in output
docs.text:
Values are converted to strings automatically:

Scheme type Output string as-is number decimal representation boolean "true" / "false" #f empty field other display representation

docs.title: Streaming with fold
docs.text:
Process rows one at a time without loading the entire file into memory:

;; Sum the third column
(define total
  (call-with-input-file "transactions.csv"
    (lambda (port)
      (csv-read-row port)  ;; skip header
      (csv-fold port
        (lambda (row acc)
          (+ acc (string->number (list-ref row 2))))
        0))))

csv-fold also supports options:

(csv-fold port proc init 'delimiter #\tab 'headers #t)

With 'headers #t, the first row is read as headers and each row passed to proc is an alist.

docs.title: Quoted fields
docs.text:
The parser handles RFC 4180 quoting automatically:

;; Fields with commas
(csv-read-string "name,bio\nAlice,\"Likes commas, really\"")
;=> (("name" "bio") ("Alice" "Likes commas, really"))

;; Fields with newlines
(csv-read-string "name,bio\nAlice,\"Line 1\nLine 2\"")
;=> (("name" "bio") ("Alice" "Line 1\nLine 2"))

;; Escaped quotes (doubled)
(csv-read-string "val\n\"She said \"\"hello\"\"\"")
;=> (("val") ("She said \"hello\""))

When writing, fields containing commas, quotes, or newlines are automatically quoted.

docs.title: Convert types
docs.text:
CSV values are always strings. Convert as needed:

(define data
  (csv-read-string "name,score\nAlice,95\nBob,87" 'headers #t))

(define scores
  (map (lambda (row)
         (string->number (cdr (assoc "score" row))))
       data))

scores  ;=> (95 87)

docs.title: Filter rows
docs.text:
(define high-scores
  (filter (lambda (row)
            (> (string->number (cdr (assoc "score" row))) 90))
          data))

docs.title: CSV to JSON
docs.text:
(import (kaappi csv) (kaappi json))

(define records
  (call-with-input-file "data.csv"
    (lambda (port) (csv-read port 'headers #t))))

(call-with-output-file "data.json"
  (lambda (port) (json-write records port)))

Header-mode rows are alists, which serialize directly to JSON objects.

docs.title: Reading
docs.text: Procedure Description (csv-read port [opts ...]) Read CSV from port (csv-read-string str [opts ...]) Read CSV from string (csv-read-row port [delim]) Single row, #f at EOF

docs.title: Writing
docs.text: Procedure Description (csv-write rows port [opts ...]) Write rows to port (csv-write-string rows [opts ...]) Write rows to string (csv-write-row row port [delim]) Write single row

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text: Procedure Description (csv-fold port proc init [opts ...]) Fold over rows

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description 'delimiter char #\, Field separator 'headers #t #f First row is headers

docs.title: Email (SMTP)
docs.text:
(kaappi email) — send email via SMTP with TLS and authentication.

thottam install kaappi-email

Depends on kaappi-net (auto-installed).

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi email))

(send-email "smtp.gmail.com" 465
  "you@gmail.com"
  '("recipient@example.com")
  "Hello from Kaappi!"
  "This email was sent from Kaappi Scheme."
  'tls #t
  'user "you@gmail.com"
  'password "your-app-password"
  'domain "gmail.com")

docs.title: send-email
docs.text:
(send-email host port from to subject body [options...])
Parameter Type Description host string SMTP server hostname port integer SMTP port (465 for TLS, 587 for STARTTLS, 25 for plain) from string Sender email address to list List of recipient email addresses subject string Email subject line body string Email body text

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Type Description 'tls boolean Use TLS connection (port 465) 'user string SMTP username for authentication 'password string SMTP password 'domain string EHLO domain 'cc list CC recipients

docs.title: Send to multiple recipients
docs.text:
(send-email "smtp.example.com" 465
  "sender@example.com"
  '("alice@example.com" "bob@example.com")
  "Team Update"
  "Here is the weekly update."
  'tls #t
  'user "sender@example.com"
  'password "password"
  'cc '("manager@example.com"))

docs.title: Message construction
docs.text:
Build messages without sending them — useful for testing or custom delivery:

(import (kaappi email mime))

(define msg
  (make-message
    "sender@example.com"
    '("recipient@example.com")
    "Test Subject"
    "Hello, world!"))

(display (message->string msg))

The (kaappi email mime) sub-library handles message construction independently from the network layer.

docs.title: Low-level SMTP
docs.text:
For full control over the SMTP session:

(import (kaappi email smtp))

(define smtp (smtp-connect-tls "smtp.gmail.com" 465))
(smtp-ehlo smtp "example.com")
(smtp-auth-plain smtp "user@gmail.com" "app-password")

(define msg (make-message "from@example.com" '("to@example.com")
              "Subject" "Body"))
(smtp-send-message smtp msg)

(smtp-close smtp)

docs.title: SMTP procedures
docs.text: Procedure Description (smtp-connect host port) Plain SMTP connection (smtp-connect-tls host port) TLS SMTP connection (smtp-ehlo smtp domain) Send EHLO greeting (smtp-auth-plain smtp user pass) PLAIN authentication (smtp-send-message smtp msg) Send a constructed message (smtp-close smtp) Close connection

docs.title: Gmail
docs.text:
Use an App Password (not your regular password):

(send-email "smtp.gmail.com" 465
  "you@gmail.com" '("to@example.com")
  "Subject" "Body"
  'tls #t
  'user "you@gmail.com"
  'password "your-16-char-app-password"
  'domain "gmail.com")

docs.title: Amazon SES
docs.text:
(send-email "email-smtp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com" 465
  "verified@yourdomain.com" '("to@example.com")
  "Subject" "Body"
  'tls #t
  'user "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
  'password "ses-smtp-password"
  'domain "yourdomain.com")

docs.title: Local / development
docs.text:
For testing without authentication:

(send-email "localhost" 1025
  "test@localhost" '("dev@localhost")
  "Test" "Testing email locally")

Use MailHog or similar for local SMTP testing.

docs.title: Sub-libraries
docs.text: Library Purpose (kaappi email) High-level send-email (kaappi email mime) Message construction (no network) (kaappi email smtp) Low-level SMTP protocol

docs.title: Utilities
docs.text: (base64-encode "hello")  ;=> "aGVsbG8="

docs.title: High-level
docs.text: Procedure Description (send-email host port from to subject body [opts]) Send email

docs.title: Message
docs.text: Procedure Description (make-message from to subject body [opts]) Create message (message->string msg) Render to MIME string

docs.title: SMTP
docs.text: Procedure Description (smtp-connect host port) Plain connection (smtp-connect-tls host port) TLS connection (smtp-ehlo smtp domain) EHLO greeting (smtp-auth-plain smtp user pass) Authenticate (smtp-send-message smtp msg) Send message (smtp-close smtp) Close connection

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Complete example applications demonstrating how to build real programs with Kaappi and its ecosystem libraries.

git clone https://github.com/kaappi/kaappi-examples
cd kaappi-examples

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Install the required libraries:

thottam install kaappi-web     # also installs kaappi-http, kaappi-json, kaappi-net
thottam install kaappi-redis
thottam install kaappi-pg

docs.title: REST API
docs.text:
Full REST API server with PostgreSQL storage, Redis caching, and JSON request/response handling via the kaappi-web framework.

Uses: kaappi-web, kaappi-pg, kaappi-redis, kaappi-json

createdb kaappi_demo
redis-server --daemonize yes
cd rest-api && kaappi app.scm

# Create a user
curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"name":"Alice","email":"alice@example.com"}' \
     http://localhost:8080/users

# List users
curl http://localhost:8080/users

# Get user (cached in Redis)
curl http://localhost:8080/users/1

docs.title: Redis Task Queue
docs.text:
Producer/consumer job queue using Redis lists.

Uses: kaappi-redis

cd redis-task-queue
kaappi app.scm producer   # enqueue 10 tasks
kaappi app.scm worker     # process all tasks
kaappi app.scm status     # show results

docs.title: PostgreSQL CRUD
docs.text:
Interactive contact book with full CRUD, search, and statistics.

Uses: kaappi-pg

cd pg-crud
createdb kaappi_demo
kaappi app.scm seed           # insert sample data
kaappi app.scm list           # list all contacts
kaappi app.scm search alice   # search by name/email
kaappi app.scm stats          # show statistics

docs.title: HTTP File Server
docs.text:
Serves static files from a directory with MIME type detection.

Uses: kaappi-http

cd http-file-server
kaappi app.scm 8080 .    # serve current directory on port 8080

docs.title: HTTP
docs.text:
(kaappi http) — HTTP/HTTPS client and HTTP server.

thottam install kaappi-http

Depends on kaappi-net (auto-installed).

docs.title: GET request
docs.text:
(import (kaappi http))

(let ((resp (http-get "https://api.github.com/"
              '(("User-Agent" . "my-app/1.0")))))
  (display (response-status resp))   ;=> 200
  (display (response-body resp)))    ;=> JSON string

docs.title: POST request
docs.text:
(let ((resp (http-post "https://httpbin.org/post"
              '(("Content-Type" . "application/json"))
              "{\"name\": \"Alice\"}")))
  (response-status resp))  ;=> 200

docs.title: All methods
docs.text:
(http-get url [headers])
(http-post url [headers] [body])
(http-put url [headers] [body])
(http-delete url [headers])
(http-head url [headers])
(http-request method url headers body)  ;; generic

HTTPS is automatic — just use https:// URLs. TLS with SNI and certificate verification via OpenSSL.

docs.title: Response accessors
docs.text:
(response-status resp)               ;=> 200
(response-reason resp)               ;=> "OK"
(response-headers resp)              ;=> (("content-type" . "application/json") ...)
(response-header resp "content-type") ;=> "application/json" or #f
(response-body resp)                 ;=> body string

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
Network errors and HTTP errors are separate concerns. Network errors (DNS failure, connection refused, TLS errors) raise Scheme errors. HTTP error status codes (4xx, 5xx) return normally — check response-status:

(let ((resp (http-get "https://httpbin.org/status/404")))
  (cond
    ((= (response-status resp) 200)
     (display "ok"))
    ((= (response-status resp) 404)
     (display "not found"))
    (else
     (display "error: ")
     (display (response-status resp)))))

For network errors, use guard:

(import (scheme base))

(guard (e (#t (display "network error\n")))
  (http-get "https://unreachable.invalid/"))

docs.title: Sending JSON
docs.text:
Combine with kaappi-json for typed request/response:

(import (kaappi http) (kaappi json))

(define (api-post url data)
  (let ((resp (http-post url
                '(("Content-Type" . "application/json")
                  ("Accept" . "application/json"))
                (json-write-string data))))
    (if (= (response-status resp) 200)
        (json-read-string (response-body resp))
        (error "API error" (response-status resp)
               (response-body resp)))))

(api-post "https://httpbin.org/post"
  '(("name" . "Alice") ("score" . 95)))

docs.title: Following redirects
docs.text:
The HTTP client does not automatically follow redirects. Check for 3xx status and re-request:

(define (http-get-follow url headers)
  (let ((resp (http-get url headers)))
    (if (and (>= (response-status resp) 300)
             (< (response-status resp) 400))
        (let ((location (response-header resp "location")))
          (if location
              (http-get-follow location headers)
              resp))
        resp)))

docs.title: Basic server
docs.text:
(import (kaappi http))

(define (handler request)
  (make-response 200 "Hello, World!"
    '(("Content-Type" . "text/plain"))))

(http-listen handler 8080)

docs.title: Request accessors
docs.text:
(request-method req)        ;=> "GET"
(request-path req)          ;=> "/api/users"
(request-query req)         ;=> "page=1&limit=10"
(request-query-params req)  ;=> (("page" . "1") ("limit" . "10"))
(request-headers req)       ;=> alist
(request-header req "host") ;=> "localhost:8080" or #f
(request-body req)          ;=> body string

docs.title: Response constructors
docs.text:
(make-response status body)
(make-response status body headers)

Headers is an alist of string pairs:

(make-response 200 "{\"ok\":true}"
  '(("Content-Type" . "application/json")
    ("Cache-Control" . "max-age=60")))

docs.title: Routing by hand
docs.text:
Without the web framework, route by matching method and path:

(define (handler req)
  (let ((method (request-method req))
        (path (request-path req)))
    (cond
      ((and (string=? method "GET") (string=? path "/"))
       (make-response 200 "Home"))
      ((and (string=? method "GET") (string=? path "/health"))
       (make-response 200 "{\"status\":\"ok\"}"
         '(("Content-Type" . "application/json"))))
      (else
       (make-response 404 "Not Found")))))

docs.title: Server modes
docs.text:
;; Sequential (one request at a time)
(http-listen handler port)
(http-listen handler port "0.0.0.0")

;; Threaded (OS thread per connection)
(http-listen-threaded handler port)

;; Pre-fork (N worker processes)
(http-listen-prefork handler port 4)

Threaded mode uses SRFI-18 OS threads, which are unavailable in --sandbox mode and in the WebAssembly build. Pre-fork mode uses fork() and is the recommended approach for production concurrency.

docs.title: URL utilities
docs.text:
(parse-url "https://example.com:8443/path?q=1")
;=> (values "https" "example.com" 8443 "/path")

Use let-values to bind the result:

(let-values (((scheme host port path)
              (parse-url "https://api.example.com/v1/users")))
  (display host))   ;=> "api.example.com"

docs.title: Query string parsing
docs.text:
(parse-query-string "name=alice&age=30")
;=> (("name" . "alice") ("age" . "30"))

(parse-query-string "q=hello+world&tag=a%26b")
;=> (("q" . "hello world") ("tag" . "a&b"))

Handles + (space) and %XX URL encoding.

docs.title: Client
docs.text: Procedure Description (http-get url [headers]) GET request (http-post url [headers] [body]) POST request (http-put url [headers] [body]) PUT request (http-delete url [headers]) DELETE request (http-head url [headers]) HEAD request (http-request method url headers body) Generic request

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Procedure Description (make-response status body [headers]) Create response (response-status resp) Status code (response-reason resp) Reason phrase (response-headers resp) All headers as alist (response-header resp name) Single header value (response-body resp) Body string

docs.title: Request
docs.text: Procedure Description (request-method req) HTTP method (request-path req) Request path (request-query req) Raw query string (request-query-params req) Parsed query params as alist (request-headers req) All headers as alist (request-header req name) Single header value (request-body req) Body string

docs.title: Server
docs.text: Procedure Description (http-listen handler port [host]) Sequential server (http-listen-threaded handler port [host]) Threaded server (http-listen-prefork handler port workers [host]) Pre-fork server

docs.title: URL
docs.text:
Procedure Description (parse-url url) Returns (values scheme host port path) (parse-query-string qs) Parse query string to alist

Use kaappi-web for routing

(kaappi http) provides the low-level HTTP primitives. For routing, middleware, and JSON helpers, use the (kaappi web) framework.

docs.title: JSON
docs.text:
(kaappi json) — JSON parser and serializer. Pure Scheme, no build step.

thottam install kaappi-json

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi json))

;; Parse JSON
(json-read-string "{\"name\": \"Alice\", \"age\": 30}")
;=> (("name" . "Alice") ("age" . 30))

;; Serialize to JSON
(json-write-string '(("name" . "Alice") ("age" . 30)))
;=> "{\"name\":\"Alice\",\"age\":30}"

docs.title: Type mapping
docs.text:
JSON Scheme Example object alist (("key" . "value")) array list (1 2 3) string string "hello" number (integer) exact integer 42 number (float) inexact number 3.14 true #t false #f null 'null symbol

Vectors are written as JSON arrays: #(1 2 3) becomes [1,2,3].

docs.title: From a string
docs.text:
(json-read-string "{\"x\": 1}")
;=> (("x" . 1))

(json-read-string "[1, 2, 3]")
;=> (1 2 3)

(json-read-string "\"hello\"")
;=> "hello"

(json-read-string "42")
;=> 42

docs.title: From a port
docs.text:
(json-read [port])

Reads from port, or current-input-port if omitted:

(call-with-input-file "config.json" json-read)

docs.title: From stdin
docs.text: echo '{"key": "value"}' | kaappi -e '(import (kaappi json)) (display (json-read))'

docs.title: To a string
docs.text:
(json-write-string '(("name" . "Alice") ("age" . 30)))
;=> "{\"name\":\"Alice\",\"age\":30}"

(json-write-string '(1 2 3))
;=> "[1,2,3]"

(json-write-string "hello")
;=> "\"hello\""

docs.title: To a port
docs.text:
(json-write val [port])

Writes to port, or current-output-port if omitted:

(call-with-output-file "output.json"
  (lambda (port)
    (json-write '(("result" . "ok")) port)))

docs.title: Access nested values
docs.text:
JSON objects are alists. Use assoc to look up keys:

(define data
  (json-read-string "{\"user\": {\"id\": 1, \"tags\": [\"admin\", \"active\"]}}"))

;; Top-level key
(cdr (assoc "user" data))
;=> (("id" . 1) ("tags" . ("admin" "active")))

;; Nested key
(cdr (assoc "id" (cdr (assoc "user" data))))
;=> 1

;; Array element
(car (cdr (assoc "tags" (cdr (assoc "user" data)))))
;=> "admin"

docs.title: Build nested structures
docs.text:
Quasiquote makes building JSON natural:

(json-write-string
  `(("users" . ,(map (lambda (name)
                       `(("name" . ,name)))
                     '("Alice" "Bob" "Charlie")))))
;=> "{\"users\":[{\"name\":\"Alice\"},{\"name\":\"Bob\"},{\"name\":\"Charlie\"}]}"

docs.title: Build from computed data
docs.text:
(define (user->json name email)
  `(("name" . ,name)
    ("email" . ,email)
    ("created" . ,(number->string (current-second)))))

(json-write-string
  `(("users" . ,(list (user->json "Alice" "alice@example.com")
                      (user->json "Bob" "bob@example.com")))))

docs.title: Null handling
docs.text:
JSON null is the symbol 'null:

(json-read-string "null")
;=> null

(json-null)
;=> null

(json-null? 'null)   ;=> #t
(json-null? "hello") ;=> #f
(json-null? #f)      ;=> #f

Emit null in output:

(json-write-string `(("value" . ,(json-null))))
;=> "{\"value\":null}"

null vs #f

JSON null is 'null (a symbol), not #f. JSON false is #f. This distinction matters when checking for missing vs. false values.

docs.title: Round-trip safety
docs.text:
Parsing and then serializing preserves structure:

(define original "{\"a\":1,\"b\":[true,null,\"x\"]}")
(define roundtrip (json-write-string (json-read-string original)))
;; roundtrip preserves all values and types

docs.title: Unicode and escape sequences
docs.text:
Full JSON spec compliance (RFC 8259):

;; Unicode escapes
(json-read-string "\"\\u0041\"")       ;=> "A"

;; Surrogate pairs (emoji)
(json-read-string "\"\\uD83D\\uDE00\"") ;=> a grinning face emoji

;; All standard escapes
(json-read-string "\"\\n\\t\\r\\\\\\\"\"")
;=> a string with newline, tab, return, backslash, quote

;; Scientific notation
(json-read-string "1.5e-3")            ;=> 0.0015
(json-read-string "2.5E+10")           ;=> 25000000000.0

docs.title: Config file loading
docs.text:
(define (load-config path)
  (call-with-input-file path json-read))

(define config (load-config "config.json"))
(define port (cdr (assoc "port" (cdr (assoc "server" config)))))

docs.title: Transform and filter
docs.text:
(define data (json-read-string "[{\"name\":\"a\",\"active\":true},{\"name\":\"b\",\"active\":false}]"))

(define active
  (filter (lambda (item) (cdr (assoc "active" item))) data))

(json-write-string active)
;=> "[{\"name\":\"a\",\"active\":true}]"

docs.title: Merge objects
docs.text:
(define (json-merge a b)
  (append b a))

(json-merge '(("x" . 1) ("y" . 2))
            '(("y" . 99) ("z" . 3)))
;=> (("y" . 99) ("z" . 3) ("x" . 1) ("y" . 2))
;; assoc finds ("y" . 99) first, so b's values take precedence

docs.title: API reference
docs.text: Procedure Description (json-read [port]) Parse JSON from port (default: stdin) (json-read-string str) Parse JSON from string (json-write val [port]) Write JSON to port (default: stdout) (json-write-string val) Write JSON to string (json-null) The null value ('null symbol) (json-null? val) Test for null

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
(kaappi log) — structured logging with levels, fields, and JSON output.

thottam install kaappi-log

Pure Scheme — no dependencies.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi log))

(log-info "server started" '("port" . 8080))
;=> 1719100000 [INFO] server started port=8080

(log-error "request failed" '("code" . 500) '("path" . "/api"))
;=> 1719100000 [ERROR] request failed code=500 path=/api

docs.title: Log levels
docs.text:
Five levels, from most to least verbose:

Level Constant Value Use for Debug LOG-DEBUG 0 Development diagnostics Info LOG-INFO 1 Normal operations Warn LOG-WARN 2 Unexpected but recoverable Error LOG-ERROR 3 Failures requiring attention Fatal LOG-FATAL 4 Unrecoverable failures 
(log-debug "loading config" '("path" . "config.json"))
(log-info "server started" '("port" . 8080))
(log-warn "slow query" '("ms" . 1500) '("table" . "users"))
(log-error "connection lost" '("host" . "db.example.com"))
(log-fatal "out of memory" '("heap" . 1073741824))

docs.title: Structured fields
docs.text:
Attach key-value pairs as '("key" . value) arguments:

(log-info "request"
  '("method" . "GET")
  '("path" . "/api/users")
  '("status" . 200)
  '("ms" . 12))

Text output:

1719100000 [INFO] request method=GET path=/api/users status=200 ms=12

Field values are converted automatically:

Scheme type Output string as-is number decimal representation boolean true / false other display representation

docs.title: Set minimum level
docs.text:
Messages below the minimum level are silently dropped:

(log-set-level! LOG-WARN)

(log-info "this is hidden")    ;; dropped
(log-warn "this is visible")   ;; printed
(log-error "this too")         ;; printed

Default minimum level is LOG-INFO.

docs.title: Set output destination
docs.text:
;; Log to a file
(log-set-port! (open-output-file "app.log"))

;; Log to stderr (default)
(log-set-port! (current-error-port))

;; Log to stdout
(log-set-port! (current-output-port))

docs.title: Switch to JSON format
docs.text:
(log-set-format! 'json)

(log-info "request" '("method" . "GET") '("status" . 200))

Output:

{"time":"1719100000","level":"INFO","msg":"request","method":"GET","status":200}

Switch back to text:

(log-set-format! 'text)

docs.title: Context fields
docs.text:
Add fields that appear on every log line within a scope:

(log-with-fields '(("req_id" . "abc-123") ("user" . "alice"))
  (lambda ()
    (log-info "loading data")
    ;=> 1719100000 [INFO] loading data req_id=abc-123 user=alice

    (log-info "query complete" '("rows" . 42))
    ;=> 1719100000 [INFO] query complete req_id=abc-123 user=alice rows=42
    ))

Context fields are prepended to per-call fields.

docs.title: Application startup logging
docs.text:
(import (kaappi log))

(log-set-format! 'json)
(log-set-level! LOG-INFO)

(log-info "starting"
  '("version" . "1.0.0")
  '("env" . "production")
  '("port" . 8080))

docs.title: Request logging in a web app
docs.text:
(define (wrap-request-logging handler)
  (lambda (request)
    (let* ((start (current-second))
           (response (handler request))
           (elapsed (- (current-second) start)))
      (log-info "request"
        `("method" . ,(request-method request))
        `("path" . ,(request-path request))
        `("status" . ,(response-status response))
        `("seconds" . ,elapsed))
      response)))

docs.title: Error logging with context
docs.text:
(guard (e (#t
           (log-error "unhandled exception"
             `("error" . ,(condition/report e))
             '("component" . "worker"))))
  (process-job job))

docs.title: Logging
docs.text: Procedure Description (log-debug msg field ...) Debug level log (log-info msg field ...) Info level log (log-warn msg field ...) Warning level log (log-error msg field ...) Error level log (log-fatal msg field ...) Fatal level log

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Procedure Description (log-set-level! level) Set minimum log level (log-set-port! port) Set output destination (log-set-format! fmt) Set format: 'text or 'json (log-with-fields fields thunk) Add context fields for scope

docs.title: Constants
docs.text: Constant Value Description LOG-DEBUG 0 Most verbose LOG-INFO 1 Default minimum LOG-WARN 2 Warnings LOG-ERROR 3 Errors LOG-FATAL 4 Unrecoverable

docs.title: Networking (TCP/TLS)
docs.text:
(kaappi net) — shared TCP client/server and TLS client. Used internally by kaappi-redis, kaappi-http, and kaappi-web.

thottam install kaappi-net

You typically don't use this library directly — the higher-level libraries handle it. But it's available for custom protocols.

docs.title: TCP Client
docs.text:
(import (kaappi net))

(let ((fd (tcp-connect "example.com" 80)))
  (let ((req (string->utf8 "GET / HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: example.com\r\n\r\n")))
    (tcp-send fd req (bytevector-length req)))
  (let ((buf (make-bytevector 4096 0)))
    (let ((n (tcp-recv fd buf 4096)))
      (display (utf8->string (bytevector-copy buf 0 n)))))
  (tcp-close fd))

docs.title: TCP Server
docs.text:
(let ((listen-fd (tcp-listen "0.0.0.0" 8080)))
  (let loop ()
    (let ((client-fd (tcp-accept listen-fd)))
      ;; handle client...
      (tcp-close client-fd)
      (loop))))

docs.title: TLS Client
docs.text:
(let ((ssl (tls-connect "api.github.com" 443)))
  (tls-send ssl request-bv (bytevector-length request-bv))
  (tls-recv ssl buf len)
  (tls-close ssl))

TLS includes SNI (Server Name Indication) and certificate verification via OpenSSL.

docs.title: TCP
docs.text: Procedure Description (tcp-connect host port [timeout]) Connect to host:port, returns fd (tcp-listen host port [backlog]) Bind + listen, returns listen fd (tcp-accept listen-fd) Accept connection, returns client fd (tcp-send fd buf len) Send bytes (tcp-recv fd buf len) Receive bytes (0 = EOF) (tcp-close fd) Close socket (tcp-last-error) Last errno

docs.title: TLS
docs.text: Procedure Description (tls-connect host port [timeout]) TLS handshake, returns handle (tls-send ssl buf len) Send over TLS (tls-recv ssl buf len) Receive over TLS (tls-close ssl) Shutdown TLS + close socket

docs.title: Non-blocking
docs.text: Procedure Description (set-nonblocking fd) Set O_NONBLOCK on socket (poll-read fd timeout-ms) 1 = ready, 0 = timeout, -1 = error (nb-accept listen-fd) Non-blocking accept (-2 = EAGAIN)

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
(kaappi pg) — PostgreSQL client with parameterized queries, cursors, transactions, and automatic type conversion.

thottam install kaappi-pg

Requires PostgreSQL client libraries (libpq) and pg_config on PATH.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi pg))

(define conn (pg-connect "host=localhost dbname=myapp"))

(pg-query conn "SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > $1" 25)
;=> (#("Alice" 30) #("Bob" 35))

(pg-close conn)

docs.title: Connection
docs.text:
(pg-connect conninfo)                           ;; libpq connection string
(pg-close conn)                                 ;; close connection
(pg-connected? conn)                            ;; check if open
(pg-error-message conn)                         ;; last error message

Connection strings use libpq format:

(pg-connect "host=localhost port=5432 dbname=myapp user=me password=secret")
(pg-connect "dbname=myapp")

docs.title: Auto-closing connection
docs.text:
(call-with-pg-connection "dbname=myapp"
  (lambda (conn)
    (pg-query conn "SELECT count(*) FROM users")))
;; connection is closed automatically

docs.title: Convenience functions
docs.text:
For most use cases, pg-query and pg-exec are all you need:

;; SELECT — returns list of row vectors
(pg-query conn "SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > $1" 25)
;=> (#("Alice" 30) #("Bob" 35))

;; INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — returns number of affected rows
(pg-exec conn "DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expired < now()")
;=> 42

(pg-exec conn "INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ($1, $2)" "Carol" 28)
;=> 1

docs.title: Parameterized queries
docs.text:
Use $1, $2, etc. for parameters (PostgreSQL native format). This prevents SQL injection:

(pg-query conn "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1 AND age > $2" "Alice" 25)
(pg-exec conn "INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ($1, $2)" "Bob" 30)

Parameters are automatically converted:

Scheme value PostgreSQL string text number text representation #t boolean true #f NULL

Use $1 parameters, not string interpolation

Never build SQL by concatenating user input. Always use $1 parameters — they prevent SQL injection and handle escaping correctly.

docs.title: Cursors
docs.text:
Cursors hold query results and provide row-by-row access. Useful for large result sets:

(define cur (pg-cursor conn))

(pg-execute cur "SELECT * FROM large_table")

(pg-fetchone cur)      ;=> #("Alice" 30) or #f at end
(pg-fetchall cur)      ;=> list of remaining row vectors
(pg-fetchmany cur 10)  ;=> up to 10 rows
(pg-description cur)   ;=> (("name" 25) ("age" 23)) — (name oid) pairs
(pg-rowcount cur)      ;=> number of rows

(pg-cursor-close cur)  ;=> free resources

docs.title: Streaming large results
docs.text:
(define cur (pg-cursor conn))
(pg-execute cur "SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY created_at")

(let loop ((row (pg-fetchone cur)))
  (when row
    (process-event row)
    (loop (pg-fetchone cur))))

(pg-cursor-close cur)

docs.title: Automatic transactions
docs.text:
call-with-pg-transaction commits on success, rolls back on error:

(call-with-pg-transaction conn
  (lambda ()
    (pg-exec conn "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = $1" 1)
    (pg-exec conn "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = $1" 2)))
;; Both updates succeed or both are rolled back

docs.title: Manual transactions
docs.text:
(pg-exec conn "BEGIN")
(pg-exec conn "INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES ($1)" "step 1")
(pg-exec conn "INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES ($1)" "step 2")
(pg-commit conn)      ;; or (pg-rollback conn) to cancel

docs.title: Type conversion
docs.text:
Results are automatically converted from PostgreSQL text format:

PostgreSQL type Scheme value boolean #t / #f smallint, integer, bigint exact integer real, double precision inexact number numeric number text, varchar string NULL #f everything else string

docs.title: Check if a row exists
docs.text: (not (null? (pg-query conn "SELECT 1 FROM users WHERE email = $1" email)))

docs.title: Count rows
docs.text:
(vector-ref (car (pg-query conn "SELECT count(*) FROM users")) 0)
;=> 42

docs.title: Insert and return the new row
docs.text:
(car (pg-query conn
  "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ($1, $2) RETURNING id, name, email"
  "Alice" "alice@example.com"))
;=> #(1 "Alice" "alice@example.com")

docs.title: Batch inserts in a transaction
docs.text:
(call-with-pg-transaction conn
  (lambda ()
    (for-each
      (lambda (user)
        (pg-exec conn "INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ($1)" user))
      '("Alice" "Bob" "Carol" "Dave"))))

docs.title: Upsert
docs.text:
(pg-exec conn
  "INSERT INTO config (key, value) VALUES ($1, $2)
   ON CONFLICT (key) DO UPDATE SET value = EXCLUDED.value"
  "theme" "dark")

docs.title: Connection pooling pattern
docs.text:
For long-running servers, keep one connection open:

(define db (pg-connect "dbname=myapp"))

;; In request handlers:
(GET "/users"
  (lambda (req params)
    (json-response (pg-query db "SELECT * FROM users"))))

For higher concurrency with serve-prefork, each worker process gets its own connection automatically (via fork()).

docs.title: Connection
docs.text: Procedure Description (pg-connect conninfo) Connect to PostgreSQL (pg-close conn) Close connection (pg-connected? conn) Check if connected (pg-error-message conn) Last error message (pg-commit conn) Commit transaction (pg-rollback conn) Rollback transaction (call-with-pg-connection conninfo proc) Auto-closing connection (call-with-pg-transaction conn proc) Auto-commit/rollback

docs.title: Convenience
docs.text: Procedure Description (pg-query conn sql arg ...) Execute + fetchall (pg-exec conn sql arg ...) Execute non-SELECT, returns rowcount

docs.title: Cursor
docs.text: Procedure Description (pg-cursor conn) Create cursor (pg-execute cur sql arg ...) Execute with $1 params (pg-fetchone cur) Next row as vector, #f at end (pg-fetchall cur) All remaining rows (pg-fetchmany cur n) Up to N rows (pg-description cur) Column info: ((name oid) ...) (pg-rowcount cur) Rows affected/returned (pg-cursor-close cur) Free resources

docs.title: Redis
docs.text:
(kaappi redis) — Redis client with strings, lists, hashes, sets, sorted sets, pub/sub, and pipelining.

thottam install kaappi-redis

Depends on kaappi-net (auto-installed). Uses the RESP2 protocol (pure Scheme, no C dependencies beyond kaappi-net).

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi redis))

(define conn (redis-connect "127.0.0.1" 6379))

(redis-set conn "greeting" "hello!")
(redis-get conn "greeting")  ;=> "hello!"

(redis-disconnect! conn)

docs.title: Connection
docs.text:
(redis-connect host port)              ;; connect
(redis-connect host port password)     ;; connect with AUTH
(redis-disconnect! conn)               ;; close
(redis-connected? conn)                ;; check connection
(redis-ping conn)                      ;=> "PONG"

docs.title: String commands
docs.text:
(redis-set conn "key" "value")         ;=> "OK"
(redis-get conn "key")                 ;=> "value" or #f if missing
(redis-del conn "key" ...)             ;=> number deleted
(redis-exists conn "key")              ;=> #t or #f
(redis-incr conn "counter")            ;=> new value (integer)
(redis-decr conn "counter")            ;=> new value (integer)
(redis-expire conn "key" 60)           ;=> #t if timeout was set
(redis-ttl conn "key")                 ;=> seconds remaining, -1 if no expiry
(redis-keys conn "user:*")             ;=> list of matching keys
(redis-mset conn "a" "1" "b" "2")     ;=> "OK"
(redis-mget conn "a" "b")             ;=> ("1" "2")
(redis-setnx conn "key" "value")       ;=> #t if key was set (didn't exist)
(redis-setex conn "key" 60 "value")    ;=> "OK" (set with TTL in seconds)
(redis-append conn "key" " world")     ;=> new string length
(redis-strlen conn "key")              ;=> string length

docs.title: List commands
docs.text:
(redis-lpush conn "list" "a" "b" "c")  ;=> list length
(redis-rpush conn "list" "x" "y")      ;=> list length
(redis-lpop conn "list")               ;=> value or #f
(redis-rpop conn "list")               ;=> value or #f
(redis-lrange conn "list" 0 -1)        ;=> all elements
(redis-llen conn "list")               ;=> length
(redis-lindex conn "list" 0)           ;=> element at index
(redis-lset conn "list" 0 "new")       ;=> "OK"

docs.title: Hash commands
docs.text:
(redis-hset conn "hash" "field" "val") ;=> 1 (new field) or 0 (updated)
(redis-hget conn "hash" "field")       ;=> "val" or #f
(redis-hdel conn "hash" "field")       ;=> number deleted
(redis-hgetall conn "hash")            ;=> (("f1" . "v1") ("f2" . "v2"))
(redis-hexists conn "hash" "field")    ;=> #t or #f
(redis-hkeys conn "hash")             ;=> list of field names
(redis-hvals conn "hash")             ;=> list of values
(redis-hlen conn "hash")              ;=> number of fields

docs.title: Set commands
docs.text:
(redis-sadd conn "set" "a" "b" "c")   ;=> number added
(redis-srem conn "set" "a")           ;=> number removed
(redis-smembers conn "set")           ;=> list of members
(redis-sismember conn "set" "a")       ;=> #t or #f
(redis-scard conn "set")              ;=> cardinality

docs.title: Sorted set commands
docs.text:
(redis-zadd conn "zset" 1.0 "a")      ;=> 1
(redis-zrem conn "zset" "a")          ;=> number removed
(redis-zrange conn "zset" 0 -1)        ;=> list ordered by score
(redis-zscore conn "zset" "a")         ;=> score as string
(redis-zcard conn "zset")             ;=> cardinality
(redis-zrank conn "zset" "a")          ;=> 0-based rank

docs.title: Pub/Sub
docs.text:
;; Publish
(redis-publish conn "channel" "message")  ;=> number of receivers

;; Subscribe (blocking)
(redis-subscribe conn "channel"
  (lambda (channel message)
    (display message) (newline)
    #t))  ;; return #f to unsubscribe

The subscribe callback receives each message. Return #t to keep listening, #f to unsubscribe and return.

docs.title: Pipelining
docs.text:
Send multiple commands at once to reduce round trips:

(redis-pipeline conn
  '("SET" "a" "1")
  '("SET" "b" "2")
  '("GET" "a")
  '("GET" "b"))
;=> ("OK" "OK" "1" "2")

Pipelining sends all commands before reading any replies. This is much faster than individual commands when you have many operations.

docs.title: Generic command
docs.text:
For Redis commands not covered by convenience functions:

(redis-command conn "INFO" "server")
(redis-command conn "FLUSHDB")
(redis-command conn "SELECT" "1")
(redis-command conn "TYPE" "mykey")

docs.title: Reply mapping
docs.text: Redis reply Scheme value Simple string (+OK) "OK" Bulk string string Null bulk string #f Integer exact integer Array list Error raises Scheme error

docs.title: Caching with expiry
docs.text:
(define (cache-get conn key)
  (redis-get conn key))

(define (cache-set conn key value ttl-seconds)
  (redis-setex conn key ttl-seconds value))

(define (cache-or-compute conn key ttl compute)
  (or (cache-get conn key)
      (let ((val (compute)))
        (cache-set conn key val ttl)
        val)))

docs.title: Rate limiting
docs.text:
(define (rate-limited? conn client-id max-requests window-seconds)
  (let ((key (string-append "rate:" client-id)))
    (let ((count (redis-incr conn key)))
      (when (= count 1)
        (redis-expire conn key window-seconds))
      (> count max-requests))))

docs.title: Job queue
docs.text:
;; Producer
(define (enqueue conn queue job-data)
  (redis-rpush conn queue (json-write-string job-data)))

;; Consumer
(define (dequeue conn queue)
  (let ((data (redis-lpop conn queue)))
    (if data (json-read-string data) #f)))

docs.title: Session store
docs.text:
(define (session-save conn session-id data ttl)
  (redis-setex conn
    (string-append "session:" session-id)
    ttl
    (json-write-string data)))

(define (session-load conn session-id)
  (let ((data (redis-get conn (string-append "session:" session-id))))
    (if data (json-read-string data) #f)))

docs.title: Connection
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-connect host port [password]) Connect to Redis (redis-disconnect! conn) Close connection (redis-connected? conn) Check connection (redis-ping conn) Ping server

docs.title: Strings
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-get conn key) Get value (redis-set conn key value) Set value (redis-del conn key ...) Delete keys (redis-exists conn key) Check existence (redis-incr conn key) Increment (redis-decr conn key) Decrement (redis-expire conn key seconds) Set TTL (redis-ttl conn key) Get TTL (redis-keys conn pattern) Find keys (redis-mset conn k v ...) Set multiple (redis-mget conn k ...) Get multiple (redis-setnx conn key value) Set if not exists (redis-setex conn key seconds value) Set with TTL (redis-append conn key value) Append to string (redis-strlen conn key) String length

docs.title: Lists
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-lpush conn key val ...) Push to head (redis-rpush conn key val ...) Push to tail (redis-lpop conn key) Pop from head (redis-rpop conn key) Pop from tail (redis-lrange conn key start stop) Get range (redis-llen conn key) List length (redis-lindex conn key index) Get by index (redis-lset conn key index value) Set by index

docs.title: Hashes
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-hset conn key field value) Set field (redis-hget conn key field) Get field (redis-hdel conn key field) Delete field (redis-hgetall conn key) Get all as alist (redis-hexists conn key field) Check field (redis-hkeys conn key) All field names (redis-hvals conn key) All values (redis-hlen conn key) Field count

docs.title: Sets
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-sadd conn key val ...) Add members (redis-srem conn key val) Remove member (redis-smembers conn key) All members (redis-sismember conn key val) Check membership (redis-scard conn key) Cardinality

docs.title: Sorted sets
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-zadd conn key score val) Add with score (redis-zrem conn key val) Remove (redis-zrange conn key start stop) Range by rank (redis-zscore conn key val) Get score (redis-zcard conn key) Cardinality (redis-zrank conn key val) Get rank

docs.title: Pub/Sub and pipeline
docs.text: Procedure Description (redis-publish conn channel message) Publish (redis-subscribe conn channel callback) Subscribe (blocking) (redis-pipeline conn cmd ...) Pipeline commands (redis-command conn cmd arg ...) Generic command

docs.title: SQLite
docs.text:
(kaappi sqlite) — SQLite database client with cursors and transactions.

thottam install kaappi-sqlite

Requires: libsqlite3 (macOS: included, Linux: apt install libsqlite3-dev)

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi sqlite))

(call-with-sqlite ":memory:"
  (lambda (db)
    (sqlite-exec db "CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)")
    (sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (?)" "Alice")
    (sqlite-query db "SELECT * FROM users")))
;=> (#(1 "Alice"))

docs.title: Connection
docs.text:
(sqlite-open path)             ;; ":memory:" for in-memory database
(sqlite-close conn)
(sqlite-open? conn)
(sqlite-error-message conn)    ;; last error message string

docs.title: Auto-closing connection
docs.text:
(call-with-sqlite "app.db"
  (lambda (db)
    (sqlite-query db "SELECT count(*) FROM users")))
;; connection is closed automatically when the lambda returns

call-with-sqlite also accepts an existing open connection — it passes it through without closing.

docs.title: Convenience functions
docs.text:
For most use cases, sqlite-query and sqlite-exec are all you need:

;; SELECT — returns list of row vectors
(sqlite-query db "SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > ?" 25)
;=> (#(1 "Alice" 30) #(2 "Bob" 28))

;; INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — returns number of affected rows
(sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES (?, ?)" "Carol" 22)
;=> 1

(sqlite-exec db "DELETE FROM users WHERE age < ?" 25)
;=> 3

;; Last inserted row ID
(sqlite-last-insert-id db)
;=> 4

docs.title: Parameterized queries
docs.text:
Use ? placeholders. Types are preserved:

(sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO t VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)" 42 3.14 "text" #f)
Scheme value SQLite type exact integer INTEGER inexact number REAL string TEXT #f NULL

docs.title: Reading results
docs.text:
Rows are returned as vectors. Column values are converted automatically:

SQLite type Scheme value INTEGER exact integer REAL inexact number TEXT string NULL #f BLOB string 
(define rows (sqlite-query db "SELECT id, name, score FROM scores"))
;; rows = (#(1 "Alice" 95.5) #(2 "Bob" 87.0))

(vector-ref (car rows) 0)   ;=> 1 (integer)
(vector-ref (car rows) 1)   ;=> "Alice" (string)
(vector-ref (car rows) 2)   ;=> 95.5 (inexact)

docs.title: Cursors
docs.text:
For streaming results or fine-grained control:

(define cur (sqlite-cursor db))

(sqlite-execute cur "SELECT * FROM large_table")

;; One row at a time
(sqlite-fetchone cur)    ;=> #(1 "Alice") or #f at end

;; All remaining rows
(sqlite-fetchall cur)    ;=> list of vectors

;; Column names
(sqlite-description cur) ;=> (("id") ("name") ("age"))

;; Rows affected by last execute
(sqlite-rowcount cur)    ;=> number

;; Free resources
(sqlite-cursor-close cur)

docs.title: Streaming large results
docs.text:
Process rows one at a time without loading everything into memory:

(define cur (sqlite-cursor db))
(sqlite-execute cur "SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY timestamp")

(let loop ((row (sqlite-fetchone cur)))
  (when row
    (process-event row)
    (loop (sqlite-fetchone cur))))

(sqlite-cursor-close cur)

docs.title: Automatic transactions
docs.text:
call-with-sqlite-transaction commits on success, rolls back on error:

(call-with-sqlite-transaction db
  (lambda ()
    (sqlite-exec db "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = ?" 1)
    (sqlite-exec db "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = ?" 2)))
;; Both updates succeed or both are rolled back

If the lambda raises an error, the transaction is rolled back and the error is re-raised.

docs.title: Manual transactions
docs.text:
(sqlite-exec db "BEGIN")
(sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES (?)" "step 1")
(sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES (?)" "step 2")
(sqlite-exec db "COMMIT")
;; or (sqlite-exec db "ROLLBACK") to cancel

docs.title: Create table if not exists
docs.text:
(sqlite-exec db "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  email TEXT UNIQUE,
  created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
)")

docs.title: Upsert
docs.text:
(sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO config (key, value) VALUES (?, ?)
  ON CONFLICT(key) DO UPDATE SET value = excluded.value"
  "theme" "dark")

docs.title: Count rows
docs.text:
(vector-ref (car (sqlite-query db "SELECT count(*) FROM users")) 0)
;=> 42

docs.title: Check if a row exists
docs.text: (not (null? (sqlite-query db "SELECT 1 FROM users WHERE email = ?" email)))

docs.title: Batch inserts in a transaction
docs.text:
(call-with-sqlite-transaction db
  (lambda ()
    (for-each
      (lambda (user)
        (sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (?)" user))
      '("Alice" "Bob" "Carol" "Dave"))))

Wrapping batch inserts in a transaction is much faster than individual commits — SQLite commits once instead of once per row.

docs.title: Connection
docs.text: Procedure Description (sqlite-open path) Open database (sqlite-close conn) Close connection (sqlite-open? conn) Check if open (sqlite-error-message conn) Last error message (call-with-sqlite path proc) Auto-closing connection

docs.title: Convenience
docs.text: Procedure Description (sqlite-exec conn sql param ...) Execute, return affected row count (sqlite-query conn sql param ...) Execute, return all rows as vectors (sqlite-last-insert-id conn) Last inserted ROWID

docs.title: Cursor
docs.text: Procedure Description (sqlite-cursor conn) Create cursor (sqlite-execute cur sql param ...) Execute with parameters (sqlite-fetchone cur) Next row as vector, #f at end (sqlite-fetchall cur) All remaining rows (sqlite-description cur) Column names (sqlite-rowcount cur) Rows affected (sqlite-cursor-close cur) Free resources

docs.title: Transactions
docs.text: Procedure Description (call-with-sqlite-transaction conn proc) Auto-commit/rollback

docs.title: Templates
docs.text:
(kaappi template) — text and HTML template engine with variables, conditionals, loops, and auto-escaping.

thottam install kaappi-template

Pure Scheme — no dependencies.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi template))

;; Plain text
(template-render "Hello, {{.name}}!" '(("name" . "Alice")))
;=> "Hello, Alice!"

;; HTML (auto-escapes <>&"')
(template-render-html "<p>{{.content}}</p>"
  '(("content" . "<script>alert('xss')</script>")))
;=> "<p><script>alert('xss')</script></p>"

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
{{.name}}           key from the data alist
{{.user.email}}     nested dotted path
{{.}}               current value (inside range loops)

Undefined keys render as empty strings.

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text:
{{if .logged_in}}Welcome back!{{end}}

{{if .logged_in}}
  Welcome, {{.username}}!
{{else}}
  Please log in.
{{end}}

Falsy values: #f, 'null, "", (), 0. Everything else is truthy.

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
{{range .items}}
  {{.name}}: ${{.price}}
{{end}}

Inside range, {{.}} refers to the current item. Use {{.field}} to access fields when items are objects (alists).

docs.title: Loop over a flat list
docs.text:
(template-render "{{range .names}}Hello {{.}}! {{end}}"
  '(("names" . ("Alice" "Bob" "Carol"))))
;=> "Hello Alice! Hello Bob! Hello Carol! "

docs.title: Loop over objects
docs.text:
(template-render "{{range .users}}{{.name}} ({{.role}})\n{{end}}"
  '(("users" . ((("name" . "Alice") ("role" . "admin"))
                 (("name" . "Bob") ("role" . "user"))))))
;=> "Alice (admin)\nBob (user)\n"

docs.title: HTML escaping
docs.text:
template-render-html escapes these characters in all variable output:

Character Escape < < > > & & " " ' '

This prevents XSS attacks. Use template-render (plain text) only when the output is not HTML.

docs.title: Data format
docs.text:
Data is passed as an alist. Nested data uses nested alists:

(define data
  '(("user" . (("name" . "Alice")
               ("email" . "alice@example.com")))
    ("items" . ((("name" . "Widget") ("price" . 9.99))
                (("name" . "Gadget") ("price" . 24.50))))
    ("show_footer" . #t)))

Template:

{{.user.name}} ({{.user.email}})

Items:
{{range .items}}
- {{.name}}: ${{.price}}
{{end}}

{{if .show_footer}}Generated by Kaappi{{end}}

docs.title: Pre-parsing for performance
docs.text:
Parse a template once, render many times:

(define tmpl (template-parse "Hello, {{.name}}!"))

(template-execute tmpl '(("name" . "Alice")) values)
;=> "Hello, Alice!"

(template-execute tmpl '(("name" . "Bob")) values)
;=> "Hello, Bob!"

The third argument to template-execute is the escape function: - values — no escaping (identity function) - html-escape — HTML entity escaping

docs.title: Performance tip
docs.text:
For templates rendered on every HTTP request, parse once at startup:

(define page-ast (template-parse page-template))

(GET "/"
  (lambda (req params)
    (html-response
      (template-execute page-ast data html-escape))))

docs.title: HTML escaping utility
docs.text:
The html-escape function is available independently:

(html-escape "<script>alert('xss')</script>")
;=> "<script>alert('xss')</script>"

(html-escape "safe text")
;=> "safe text"

docs.title: Layout with content blocks
docs.text:
(define layout "<html><head><title>{{.title}}</title></head>
<body><nav>{{.nav}}</nav>{{.body}}</body></html>")

(define (render-page title body-html)
  (template-render-html layout
    `(("title" . ,title)
      ("nav" . "<a href='/'>Home</a>")
      ("body" . ,body-html))))

docs.title: Conditional CSS classes
docs.text:
<div class="alert {{if .error}}alert-error{{else}}alert-info{{end}}">
  {{.message}}
</div>

docs.title: Empty state handling
docs.text:
{{if .items}}
<ul>
  {{range .items}}<li>{{.name}}</li>{{end}}
</ul>
{{else}}
<p>No items found.</p>
{{end}}

docs.title: API reference
docs.text: Procedure Description (template-render tmpl data) Render with no escaping (template-render-html tmpl data) Render with HTML escaping (template-parse tmpl) Parse to reusable AST (template-execute ast data escape-fn) Execute AST with custom escaping (html-escape str) Escape HTML entities

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
(kaappi test) — test framework with groups, assertions, and reporting.

thottam install kaappi-test

Pure Scheme — no dependencies. SRFI-64 inspired.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi test))

(test-group "arithmetic"
  (test-equal "2+2" 4 (+ 2 2))
  (test-assert "positive" (> 5 0)))

(test-group "errors"
  (test-error "type error" (lambda () (+ 1 "two"))))

(test-exit)

Run it:

kaappi tests/test-math.scm

Output:

arithmetic
  ok: 2+2
  ok: positive
errors
  ok: type error

3 tests: 3 passed

test-exit prints the summary and exits with code 1 if any test failed.

docs.title: test-equal — deep structural equality
docs.text:
Uses equal? to compare. Works with lists, vectors, strings, numbers:

(test-equal "lists match" '(1 2 3) (list 1 2 3))
(test-equal "strings" "hello" (string-append "hel" "lo"))
(test-equal "vectors" #(1 2) (vector 1 2))
(test-equal "nested" '((a . 1)) (list (cons 'a 1)))

On failure, shows expected and actual values:

  FAIL: lists match
    expected: (1 2 3)
    actual:   (1 2 4)

docs.title: test-eqv — value identity
docs.text:
Uses eqv?. Best for numbers, characters, and booleans:

(test-eqv "exact number" 42 (* 6 7))
(test-eqv "character" #\a (string-ref "abc" 0))
(test-eqv "boolean" #t (pair? '(1)))

docs.title: test-assert — truthiness
docs.text:
Passes if the expression is any non-#f value:

(test-assert "positive" (> 5 0))
(test-assert "string check" (string? "hello"))
(test-assert "list is pair" (pair? '(1 2 3)))

docs.title: test-not — falsiness
docs.text:
Passes if the expression is #f:

(test-not "empty is not pair" (pair? '()))
(test-not "zero is not positive" (> 0 5))

docs.title: test-approximate — floating point tolerance
docs.text:
Passes if |expected - actual| <= tolerance:

(test-approximate "pi" 3.14159 (acos -1) 0.001)
(test-approximate "sqrt2" 1.414 (sqrt 2) 0.001)

docs.title: test-error — expected errors
docs.text:
Passes if the thunk raises an error:

(test-error "division by zero" (lambda () (/ 1 0)))
(test-error "type error" (lambda () (+ 1 "two")))
(test-error "out of range" (lambda () (vector-ref #(1 2) 5)))

The thunk must be a zero-argument lambda — it is called inside a guard that catches the error.

docs.title: Test groups
docs.text:
Groups organize tests into labeled sections. They nest:

(test-group "parser"
  (test-group "numbers"
    (test-equal "integer" 42 (parse-int "42"))
    (test-equal "negative" -7 (parse-int "-7")))

  (test-group "strings"
    (test-equal "simple" "hello" (parse-str "\"hello\""))
    (test-equal "empty" "" (parse-str "\"\""))))

Output:

parser
  numbers
    ok: integer
    ok: negative
  strings
    ok: simple
    ok: empty

4 tests: 4 passed

docs.title: Suppress passing tests
docs.text:
For large suites, show only failures and the summary:

(test-verbose! #f)

docs.title: Get test counts
docs.text:
(test-pass-count)   ;=> number of passed tests
(test-fail-count)   ;=> number of failed tests
(test-skip-count)   ;=> number of skipped tests

docs.title: Testing a library
docs.text:
Typical project layout:

my-lib/
  lib/
    my-lib/
      parser.sld
  tests/
    test-parser.scm

tests/test-parser.scm:

(import (scheme base)
        (kaappi test)
        (my-lib parser))

(test-group "parser"
  (test-equal "parse integer" 42 (parse "42"))
  (test-error "bad input" (lambda () (parse "???"))))

(test-exit)

Run from the project root:

kaappi tests/test-parser.scm

docs.title: Setup and teardown
docs.text:
Use let bindings inside groups for per-group setup:

(import (kaappi test) (kaappi sqlite))

(test-group "database"
  (let ((db (sqlite-open ":memory:")))
    (sqlite-exec db "CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)")

    (test-equal "insert"
      1 (sqlite-exec db "INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (?)" "Alice"))

    (test-equal "query"
      '#(1 "Alice")
      (car (sqlite-query db "SELECT * FROM users")))

    (sqlite-close db)))

docs.title: Checking procedure coverage
docs.text:
After writing tests, verify all exported procedures are exercised:

kaappi --coverage --lib-path ./lib tests/test-parser.scm

Coverage:
  (my-lib parser)    5/5  100.0%
  Overall: 5/5 procedures covered (100.0%)

Uncalled procedures are listed by name. For CI, generate Cobertura XML:

kaappi --coverage-xml coverage.xml --lib-path ./lib tests/test-parser.scm

docs.title: Running in CI
docs.text:
name: Tests
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install Kaappi
        run: curl -fsSL https://kaappi-lang.org/install.sh | bash
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: thottam install kaappi-test
      - name: Run tests
        run: kaappi tests/test-all.scm

docs.title: Running multiple test files
docs.text:
Create a runner that loads all test files:

;; tests/test-all.scm
(import (kaappi test))

(load "tests/test-parser.scm")
(load "tests/test-validator.scm")

(test-exit)

Or aggregate in the shell:

kaappi tests/test-parser.scm && kaappi tests/test-validator.scm

docs.title: Groups
docs.text: Procedure Description (test-group name body ...) Labeled test group (test-begin name) Start group (low-level) (test-end) End group (low-level)

docs.title: Assertions
docs.text: Procedure Description (test-equal name expected actual) Deep equality (equal?) (test-eqv name expected actual) Value identity (eqv?) (test-assert name expr) Truthy check (test-not name expr) Falsy check (test-approximate name expected actual tol) Within tolerance (test-error name thunk) Expects error

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text: Procedure Description (test-exit) Print summary, exit 1 on failure (test-verbose! bool) Show/hide passing tests (test-pass-count) Number of passed tests (test-fail-count) Number of failed tests (test-skip-count) Number of skipped tests

docs.title: thottam — Package Manager
docs.text: thottam installs, updates, and removes Kaappi ecosystem libraries. It is a compiled Zig binary that ships alongside kaappi in release artifacts for all platforms (macOS, Linux x86_64, Linux aarch64, Linux riscv64).

docs.title: Getting thottam
docs.text:
thottam is installed automatically by the Kaappi installer:

curl -fsSL https://kaappi-lang.org/install.sh | bash

If you build from source, zig build produces both kaappi and thottam in zig-out/bin/.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
thottam install <pkg>[@<ver>][::url]   # Install a package (optionally pinned/sourced)
thottam remove <package>               # Remove a package
thottam list                           # List installed packages
thottam update [package]               # Update one or all packages
thottam verify                         # Check installed packages match lockfile

docs.title: Install a package
docs.text:
thottam install kaappi-web

This will:

  1. Clone github.com/kaappi/kaappi-web to ~/.kaappi/src/kaappi-web
  2. Read kaappi.pkg for dependencies (kaappi-http, kaappi-json)
  3. Recursively install each dependency
  4. Build if needed (make)
  5. Copy .sld files and native libraries to ~/.kaappi/lib/

docs.title: Auto-discovery
docs.text:
After installation, Kaappi automatically finds libraries in ~/.kaappi/lib/. No --lib-path flags or environment variables needed:

# Before thottam:
DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH=../kaappi-net:../kaappi-http \
kaappi --lib-path ../kaappi-net/lib \
       --lib-path ../kaappi-http/lib \
       --lib-path ../kaappi-json/lib \
       app.scm

# After thottam:
kaappi app.scm

docs.title: List installed packages
docs.text:
$ thottam list
Installed packages:
  kaappi-net
  kaappi-http (depends: kaappi-net)
  kaappi-json
  kaappi-web (depends: kaappi-http kaappi-json)

docs.title: Update packages
docs.text:
thottam update              # update all installed packages
thottam update kaappi-web   # update a specific package

docs.title: Remove a package
docs.text: thottam remove kaappi-web

docs.title: Version pinning
docs.text:
Pin a package to a specific tag or commit:

thottam install kaappi-web@v1.0.0
thottam install kaappi-http@abc1234

The resolved commit SHA is recorded in the lockfile (~/.kaappi/thottam.lock).

docs.title: Version constraints
docs.text:
Use semantic versioning constraints to install the latest compatible version:

thottam install kaappi-net@">=0.2.0"        # 0.2.0 or newer
thottam install kaappi-http@"^0.3.0"        # compatible (same major: 0.3.x)
thottam install kaappi-web@"~1.2.0"         # patch-level (1.2.x)
thottam install kaappi-pg@">=1.0.0,<2.0.0"  # range
Operator Meaning Example >= Greater than or equal >=1.0.0 matches 1.0.0, 1.5.0, 2.0.0 > Greater than >1.0.0 matches 1.0.1 but not 1.0.0 <= Less than or equal <=2.0.0 matches 1.9.9, 2.0.0 < Less than <2.0.0 matches 1.9.9 but not 2.0.0 ^ Compatible (same major) ^0.3.0 matches 0.3.0, 0.4.0 but not 1.0.0 ~ Patch-level (same major.minor) ~1.2.0 matches 1.2.0, 1.2.5 but not 1.3.0

Constraints resolve against git tags via git ls-remote --tags. The latest matching version is selected. Constraints can also be used in kaappi.pkg manifests:

depends: kaappi-net@">=0.2.0" kaappi-json@"^0.1.0"

docs.title: Custom source URLs
docs.text:
Install a package from any Git URL using the :: syntax:

thottam install kaappi-auth::https://github.com/bob/kaappi-auth
thottam install kaappi-http@v1.1::https://github.com/alice/kaappi-http

Without ::, packages are fetched from KAAPPI_ORG (default: https://github.com/kaappi). Custom URLs are recorded in the lockfile so thottam update pulls from the correct source.

Dependencies can also specify source URLs in kaappi.pkg:

name: my-app
depends: kaappi-web kaappi-auth::https://github.com/bob/kaappi-auth

docs.title: Lockfile and reproducible installs
docs.text:
Every thottam install records the exact commit SHA in ~/.kaappi/thottam.lock. Use --locked to enforce the lockfile in CI or production -- it refuses to install any package not already locked:

thottam --locked install kaappi-web   # fails if kaappi-web is not in lockfile

In --locked mode, the resolved SHA is also verified against the lockfile entry. A mismatch aborts the install.

docs.title: Verify integrity
docs.text:
Check that all installed packages match their locked SHAs:

thottam verify

docs.title: Installation layout
docs.text:
~/.kaappi/
├── lib/                    # .sld files + native libraries
│   ├── kaappi/
│   │   ├── net.sld
│   │   ├── json.sld
│   │   ├── http.sld
│   │   ├── http/
│   │   │   ├── parse.sld
│   │   │   ├── client.sld
│   │   │   └── server.sld
│   │   └── web.sld
│   ├── libkaappi_net.dylib
│   └── libkaappi_pg.dylib
├── src/                    # Cloned source repos
│   ├── kaappi-net/
│   ├── kaappi-http/
│   └── ...
├── thottam.lock            # Pinned commit SHAs (and source URLs) for reproducibility
└── installed.txt           # Installed package list

docs.title: Package manifest
docs.text:
Each package has a kaappi.pkg file in its root:

name: kaappi-web
depends: kaappi-http kaappi-json
build: make
source: https://github.com/kaappi/kaappi-web
Field Required Description name yes Package name depends no Space-separated dependency specs (name[@ver][::url]) build no Build command (omit for pure Scheme) source no Git URL where this package is hosted

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Git (for cloning packages)
  • C compiler (for packages with native code, e.g. kaappi-net)
  • OpenSSL (brew install openssl / apt install libssl-dev) for kaappi-net
  • PostgreSQL client libs (brew install libpq / apt install libpq-dev) for kaappi-pg

docs.title: Shell completions
docs.text:
Enable tab completion for thottam subcommands and flags:

# Bash — add to ~/.bashrc
eval "$(thottam --completions bash)"

# Zsh — add to ~/.zshrc
eval "$(thottam --completions zsh)"

# Fish — add to ~/.config/fish/config.fish
thottam --completions fish | source

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
Variable Default Description KAAPPI_HOME ~/.kaappi Root directory for installed packages, source repos, and the lockfile KAAPPI_ORG https://github.com/kaappi Base URL for fetching packages 
# Store packages in a project-local directory
KAAPPI_HOME=./vendor thottam install kaappi-json

# Fetch packages from a private GitHub org
KAAPPI_ORG=https://github.com/my-org thottam install my-lib

docs.title: TOML
docs.text:
(kaappi toml) — TOML parser and serializer.

thottam install kaappi-toml

Pure Scheme — no dependencies.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi toml))

(define config (toml-read-string "
[server]
host = \"localhost\"
port = 8080

[database]
url = \"sqlite:///app.db\"
"))

(toml-ref* config "server" "port")   ;=> 8080

docs.title: From a string
docs.text:
(toml-read-string "key = \"value\"")
;=> (("key" . "value"))

docs.title: From a file
docs.text: (call-with-input-file "config.toml" toml-read)

docs.title: Nested access
docs.text:
Use toml-ref for single keys and toml-ref* for nested paths:

(define config (toml-read-string "
[server]
host = \"localhost\"
port = 8080
debug = true
"))

(toml-ref config "server")
;=> (("host" . "localhost") ("port" . 8080) ("debug" . #t))

(toml-ref* config "server" "host")   ;=> "localhost"
(toml-ref* config "server" "port")   ;=> 8080
(toml-ref* config "server" "debug")  ;=> #t

docs.title: Writing TOML
docs.text:
(toml-write-string
  '(("title" . "My App")
    ("server" . (("host" . "0.0.0.0")
                 ("port" . 8080)))))

(call-with-output-file "config.toml"
  (lambda (port)
    (toml-write '(("key" . "value")) port)))

docs.title: Type mapping
docs.text: TOML Scheme Example String string "hello" Integer exact integer 42 Float inexact number 3.14 Boolean #t / #f Array list (1 2 3) Table alist (("key" . "value")) Datetime string "2026-06-25T10:00:00Z"

docs.title: Supported TOML features
docs.text:
• Basic and literal strings, multiline strings
  • Integers in decimal, hex (0x), octal (0o), binary (0b), with underscores (1_000_000)
  • Floats including inf, -inf, nan
  • Booleans
  • Arrays and nested arrays
  • Tables and nested tables
  • Inline tables
  • Array of tables ([[section]])
  • Comments
  • Datetime strings (offset, local date, local time)
  • Unicode escape sequences

docs.title: Application config
docs.text:
(define (load-config path)
  (call-with-input-file path toml-read))

(define config (load-config "app.toml"))

(define db-url (toml-ref* config "database" "url"))
(define port (or (toml-ref* config "server" "port") 8080))

docs.title: Config with defaults
docs.text:
(define (config-ref config . keys)
  (let loop ((c config) (ks keys))
    (if (null? ks)
        c
        (let ((pair (assoc (car ks) c)))
          (if pair
              (loop (cdr pair) (cdr ks))
              #f)))))

(or (config-ref config "server" "port") 8080)
(or (config-ref config "server" "host") "0.0.0.0")

docs.title: API reference
docs.text: Procedure Description (toml-read [port]) Parse from port (toml-read-string str) Parse from string (toml-write table [port]) Serialize to port (toml-write-string table) Serialize to string (toml-ref table key) Lookup single key (toml-ref* table key ...) Nested key lookup

docs.title: Web Framework
docs.text:
(kaappi web) — routing, middleware, cookies, sessions, and response helpers for building web applications.

thottam install kaappi-web

Depends on kaappi-http and kaappi-json (auto-installed).

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi web))

(define app
  (routes
    (GET "/"
      (lambda (req params)
        (text-response "Hello, World!")))

    (GET "/users/:id"
      (lambda (req params)
        (json-response
          `(("id" . ,(param/number params "id"))))))

    (POST "/users"
      (lambda (req params)
        (let ((body (request-json req)))
          (json-response body 201))))))

(serve (wrap app wrap-json-body wrap-logging wrap-errors) 8080)

docs.title: Routing
docs.text:
Define routes with method + path pattern + handler:

(routes
  (GET    "/path"       handler)
  (POST   "/path"       handler)
  (PUT    "/path"       handler)
  (DELETE "/path"       handler)
  (PATCH  "/path"       handler)
  (HEAD   "/path"       handler))

The first matching route wins. If no route matches, a 404 JSON response is returned automatically.

docs.title: Path parameters
docs.text:
Use :name in path patterns to capture segments:

(GET "/users/:id"
  (lambda (req params)
    (param params "id")            ;=> "42"
    (param/number params "id")     ;=> 42
    ...))

(GET "/users/:uid/posts/:pid"
  (lambda (req params)
    ;; params = (("uid" . "5") ("pid" . "99"))
    ...))

Path matching is segment-based — :id matches exactly one path segment between / delimiters.

docs.title: Handlers
docs.text:
Every handler takes (request params) and returns a response:

(lambda (req params)
  (json-response '(("status" . "ok"))))

docs.title: Response helpers
docs.text:
Procedure Description (json-response data [status]) JSON body with application/json content type (text-response text [status]) Plain text response (html-response html [status]) HTML response (redirect url [status]) 302 redirect (or custom status) (no-content) 204 No Content

Status defaults to 200 unless specified:

(json-response '(("created" . #t)) 201)
(json-response '(("error" . "not found")) 404)
(redirect "/login" 301)

docs.title: Request utilities
docs.text:
(request-method req)             ;=> "GET"
(request-path req)               ;=> "/api/users"
(request-body req)               ;=> raw body string
(request-header req "host")      ;=> "localhost:8080" or #f
(request-query-params req)       ;=> (("page" . "1") ("limit" . "10"))

(param params "id")              ;=> path parameter or #f
(param/number params "id")       ;=> path param as number or #f
(request-json req)               ;=> parsed JSON body (set by wrap-json-body)

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text:
Middleware are (handler -> handler) functions. Compose them with wrap:

(serve
  (wrap app
    wrap-json-body
    wrap-logging
    (wrap-cors "*")
    wrap-errors)
  8080)

Middleware runs outside-in: wrap-errors catches exceptions from everything inside it.

docs.title: wrap-json-body
docs.text:
Parses application/json request bodies. Access the parsed data with (request-json req):

(POST "/api/data"
  (lambda (req params)
    (let ((body (request-json req)))
      (json-response body))))

docs.title: wrap-logging
docs.text:
Prints each request to stdout:

GET /users/42
POST /api/data

Includes method, path, and query string.

docs.title: (wrap-cors origin)
docs.text:
Adds CORS headers and handles OPTIONS preflight:

(wrap-cors "*")                 ;; allow all origins
(wrap-cors "https://myapp.com") ;; specific origin

Allows GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, and OPTIONS methods.

docs.title: wrap-errors
docs.text:
Catches unhandled exceptions and returns a 500 JSON error:

{"error": "Internal server error"}

Place wrap-errors as the outermost middleware so it catches errors from all inner layers.

docs.title: Custom middleware
docs.text:
A middleware function takes a handler and returns a new handler:

(define (wrap-auth handler)
  (lambda (request)
    (if (request-header request "authorization")
        (handler request)
        (json-response '(("error" . "Unauthorized")) 401))))

docs.title: Middleware composition example
docs.text:
(define (wrap-request-id handler)
  (lambda (request)
    (let ((id (number->string (random-integer 1000000))))
      (handler request))))

(define (wrap-timing handler)
  (lambda (request)
    (let* ((start (current-jiffy))
           (response (handler request))
           (elapsed (/ (- (current-jiffy) start)
                       (jiffies-per-second))))
      (log-info "request" `("elapsed" . ,elapsed))
      response)))

(serve
  (wrap app
    wrap-json-body
    wrap-timing
    wrap-request-id
    wrap-logging
    wrap-errors)
  8080)

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text:
Read and write HTTP cookies:

;; Read cookies from a request
(request-cookies req)           ;=> (("session" . "abc") ("theme" . "dark"))
(request-cookie req "session")  ;=> "abc" or #f

;; Set a cookie on a response
(with-cookie
  (json-response '(("ok" . #t)))
  "session" "abc123"
  '((path . "/")
    (max-age . 3600)
    (http-only . #t)
    (secure . #t)
    (same-site . "Strict")))

docs.title: Cookie options
docs.text: Option Type Description path string Cookie path (default: none) domain string Cookie domain max-age integer Seconds until expiry http-only boolean Inaccessible to JavaScript secure boolean HTTPS only same-site string "Strict", "Lax", or "None"

docs.title: Build a Set-Cookie header directly
docs.text:
(set-cookie "theme" "dark" '((path . "/") (max-age . 86400)))
;=> ("Set-Cookie" . "theme=dark; Path=/; Max-Age=86400")

docs.title: Sessions
docs.text:
Server-side sessions backed by an in-memory store, with a session ID cookie:

(import (kaappi web))

(define store (make-memory-session-store))

(define app
  (routes
    (POST "/login"
      (lambda (req params)
        (let ((body (request-json req)))
          (session-set! req "user" (cdr (assoc "username" body)))
          (json-response '(("logged_in" . #t))))))

    (GET "/profile"
      (lambda (req params)
        (let ((user (session-ref req "user")))
          (if user
              (json-response `(("user" . ,user)))
              (json-response '(("error" . "not logged in")) 401)))))

    (POST "/logout"
      (lambda (req params)
        (session-destroy! req)
        (json-response '(("logged_out" . #t)))))))

(serve
  (wrap app
    wrap-json-body
    (wrap-session store)
    wrap-errors)
  8080)

docs.title: Session API
docs.text:
(session-ref req "key")            ;=> value or #f
(session-set! req "key" value)     ;=> sets value in session
(session-delete! req "key")        ;=> removes key from session
(session-destroy! req)             ;=> clears entire session
(session-id req)                   ;=> session ID string

wrap-session middleware must be in the middleware stack for sessions to work.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Built-in authentication middleware that checks for a user key in the session:

;; Protect routes — redirects unauthenticated users to /login
(serve
  (wrap app
    wrap-json-body
    (wrap-session (make-memory-session-store))
    (wrap-auth "/login")
    wrap-errors)
  8080)

docs.title: Authentication helpers
docs.text:
(authenticated? req)    ;=> #t if session has "user" key
(current-user req)      ;=> session "user" value or #f

wrap-auth accepts either a redirect URL string or a handler function for custom unauthorized responses:

;; Redirect to login page
(wrap-auth "/login")

;; Return JSON error
(wrap-auth (lambda (req)
  (json-response '(("error" . "authentication required")) 401)))

docs.title: Server modes
docs.text:
(serve app 8080)                    ;; sequential
(serve app 8080 "0.0.0.0")         ;; explicit bind address
(serve-prefork app 8080 4)          ;; 4 pre-forked worker processes

serve-prefork forks worker processes that share the listen socket. Each worker handles requests independently. Good for CPU-bound workloads.

docs.title: Routing
docs.text: Procedure Description (routes route ...) Combine routes into a handler (GET pattern handler) GET route (POST pattern handler) POST route (PUT pattern handler) PUT route (DELETE pattern handler) DELETE route (PATCH pattern handler) PATCH route (HEAD pattern handler) HEAD route

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Procedure Description (json-response data [status]) JSON response (text-response text [status]) Plain text response (html-response html [status]) HTML response (redirect url [status]) Redirect (default 302) (no-content) 204 No Content

docs.title: Request
docs.text: Procedure Description (param params name) Path parameter by name (param/number params name) Path parameter as number (request-json req) Parsed JSON body (request-cookies req) All cookies as alist (request-cookie req name) Single cookie value

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text: Procedure Description (set-cookie name value [opts]) Build Set-Cookie header pair (with-cookie response name value [opts]) Add cookie to response

docs.title: Sessions
docs.text: Procedure Description (make-memory-session-store) Create in-memory session store (wrap-session handler [store]) Session middleware (session-ref req key) Get session value (session-set! req key value) Set session value (session-delete! req key) Remove session key (session-destroy! req) Clear entire session (session-id req) Get session ID

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Procedure Description (authenticated? req) Check if user is in session (current-user req) Get user from session (wrap-auth handler-or-url) Auth guard middleware

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text: Procedure Description (wrap handler mw ...) Compose middleware wrap-json-body Parse JSON request bodies wrap-logging Log requests to stdout (wrap-cors origin) Add CORS headers wrap-errors Catch exceptions, return 500

docs.title: Server
docs.text: Procedure Description (serve handler port [host]) Start server (serve-prefork handler port workers [host]) Start prefork server

docs.title: YAML
docs.text:
(kaappi yaml) — YAML parser and writer.

thottam install kaappi-yaml

Pure Scheme — no dependencies.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
(import (kaappi yaml))

(define config (yaml-read-string "
server:
  host: localhost
  port: 8080
routes:
  - /
  - /api
"))

(yaml-ref* config "server" "port")   ;=> 8080
(yaml-ref config "routes")           ;=> ("/" "/api")

docs.title: From a string
docs.text:
(yaml-read-string "key: value")
;=> (("key" . "value"))

docs.title: From a file
docs.text: (call-with-input-file "config.yml" yaml-read)

docs.title: Nested access
docs.text:
(define config (yaml-read-string "
app:
  name: My Service
  version: 1.2.0
  features:
    - auth
    - logging
"))

(yaml-ref config "app")
;=> (("name" . "My Service") ("version" . "1.2.0") ("features" . ("auth" "logging")))

(yaml-ref* config "app" "name")      ;=> "My Service"
(yaml-ref* config "app" "features")  ;=> ("auth" "logging")

docs.title: Writing YAML
docs.text:
(yaml-write-string
  '(("name" . "My App")
    ("version" . "1.0.0")
    ("features" . ("auth" "logging"))))
